speaker: interviewer
text: --1996. Interview with Mr. William Cohn. Dallas, Texas, United States of America. Mr. Cohn, you were speaking regarding experiences with antisemitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, antisemitism was rampant always in Germany. And not only in Germany, but in-- was very widespread in Europe, Western and Eastern Europe. Eastern Europe, it was worse, worse yet. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] our customers were mostly working class people and, and left-wing, organized, unionized, and organized unions and, and belonging to left-wing parties. And they were not antisemitic. Antisemitism was mainly in the middle classes, middle and upper classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] of course, in the-- in the business circles of the town, there were a few Jewish people that had businesses. And so they were-- of course, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was always comp-- anyway, businesses, competition anyway. But, but most of those small storekeepers, shopkeepers, were, were right-wing people, you know, right-wing lawyers and-- and [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] what was your question? Did you have other experiences with antisemitism yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] One time, I was in, in some kind of a rally in a, in a-- in the center of the town, in a marketplace. I don't know-- I don't remember what it was. And somebody came up to me and started-- wanted to hit me in the face. And though there was another man, another guy, older than I-- not a student. There were all sorts of people there, and I didn't know the guy who wanted to hit me. And, and he, the other-- the other fellow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prevented it. He held him back, and he was-- he was an SR, SR man. You know, is from, from the Nazi Stormtroopers. And he held him back. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never knew why. But we were kind of respected by people there, in spite of being Jewish. But-- personally. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then at school, also one time I found in my pocket a sort of a train-- train ticket. And it said, one-way ticket to Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, that means, go to Palestine, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And other arguments all the time, you know, about politics also, and about Jews. And anything specific, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I really don't recall. But, yeah, my uncle, who went to the same school, the-- my father's brother, he, he-- my father told me that often he came from school with broken eyeglasses because he had to fight with-- with other students that harassed him as a Jew. So he really felt it. And-- so it was before the First World War. And, well, afterwards, of course, politics changed, you know, after the First World War. And the-- Germany became a republic, a democratic republic. So-- and the, as I-- as I said before, the Democrats were mostly not antisemitic. And the working class was not. And they were the majority in, in the republic-- at first. But afterwards, of course, because of the-- of the economic deterioration after the First World War, the Nazis were founded in the first place. And then because of the economic [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] distress, you know, and, and unemployment, very high unemployment and enormous-- I mean, astronomic inflation, you know-- when the money completely lost its value and people lost their savings all because of the war, the First World War, which-- which Germany started.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Nazis really grew. And more and more antisemitism grew with it because it was always a very effective means of, of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] getting the support of many citizens for, for the nationalistic parties. Were you in any fights in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in any fights in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Identified?

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned fights earlier. Were you in any fights with any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. It never went that far. It always remained on the level of arguments or-- yeah, arguments. And some-- oh, no. And we didn't-- I don't remember altogether that-- that there were fights in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just had one, one friend all the time, from-- I think from kindergarten on. He was not Jewish. And we kind of drifted apart afterwards when the high schools-- those high schools-- after third-- after three classes-- the first three classes, they, they were-- they were divided into-- what's it called? Realistic high school and humanistic high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were different programs. One was more based on, on mathematics and physics and modern, modern languages like English, mainly English, I think. French-- no, I don't think. And the other one, the humanistic, was more based on classics and on liberal arts. And I went to that, to liberal arts. And my friend went to the realistic. So that's when we kind of drifted apart. What was your friend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fritzian Westendorf. [LAUGHS] Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. I remember for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when, when I came to his home, they were-- they were actually quite impoverished. Bourgeois, but impoverished. Impoverished bourgeois.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and I came to their house on Christmas. I still remember the beautiful Christmas tree they always had there, you know? And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] yeah, we were-- we were very close until we drifted apart. And after that I didn't have any personal, close friends at all. What kind of things did you do together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we took our bicycles and-- at first, when we were little kids, you know, we-- there was-- in the-- in the center of the town there was a Roland. You know what a Roland is? It's an, an, an, an ancient Teutonic thing, musical, musical figure, hero. And many towns had a, had a, had a statue of this figure. And we also in our town-- a tall, big, big figure. From the Middle Ages, you know. [INAUDIBLE] from the Middle Ages, kind of in the Gothic, Gothic style, kind of. And we played around that. Yeah. And the church. There was a big church near to it. It still is there. Both of them are still there. And we played with our rackets. Indians and cowboys, or something. You know, some games like this, you know. And, and then otherwise we, you know, we came to each others houses and played with soldiers and all these-- children's, children's games, you know. And then when we were older, we took our bikes and, and and rode it into the forests and out into the country. And so, yeah, that's about-- yeah. These things. And then girls came in between. You know, his-- he had a sister, and his sister was a friend of my sister. And so that was about it. Yeah. And my mother-- yeah, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were-- my mother worked, as I said, with my father in his store. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were not, not, not very religious. We were-- even as far as for the High Holy Days, you know, we went to the synagogue. We had a synagogue. And there were about 13 Jewish families in the whole town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had a rabbi, a Reform rabbi. And for the High Holy Days, we went to church-- to, to temple-- to the temple. And I remember my father with a big top hat, you know? And-- but I was always bored. And one time I remember with the other-- with one of the other Jewish boys on Yom Kippur when you had-- you have to fast, you know, and be all day long in the synagogue and pray, we sneaked out. And there was a, a big-- a factory that had burned down. And so we went and, and, and searched for-- for metal for, for, for, you know, metal, metal pieces to sell them, to, to sell it to the-- to a, to a, a metal place, you know, where all the metal is being-- old metal and old materials are being taken and, and eventually be recycled. Yeah? So we did that and-- instead of being in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went there and, and-- to look for metal. But then I-- then I-- then we kept it at home, and-- and I was always kind of concerned about it. [LAUGHS] I, I don't think we ever sold it. But so we were not very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: My parent were Reform. Reform, kind of. But my mother, she had her own personal kind of religion. She took, on, on, on Saturdays, on Shabbat, yes? She left the store, went upstairs to our apartment, and she sat in a, in a corner of one room with a prayer book. And she prayed there, sat there and prayed by herself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her, her-- she was-- she had-- she wasn't-- in that way she was religious. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was more a really deep feeling, you know, than a ritual. You had mentioned earlier about your family's store. What kind of store was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mostly clothing and-- for working people. Working clothes. And also suits and pants and stuff, and, and shoes. And we also had-- yeah, we also had another building that belonged to my parents. Four houses in the same street far down. We had a furniture, furniture factory, factory. And just-- factory and store. And I spent a lot of time, yeah, and with a, with a master carpenter. And-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I kind of developed a kind of liking for-- for wood, you know, for wood things and so on. And I became kind of friends with a master carpenter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you work at the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you work at the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did you?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you work at the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't work at the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't work at the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just went-- just went-- just went [BACKGROUND NOISE] to, to, to spend time there. To look, to look, to watch the carpenters, you know. And my father built another building in the back of it, three-story building with, with machinery. And I was always interested in that. I always liked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned your parents. Can you tell me a little bit about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I said, I was very close with her. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] once I remember-- you know, [INAUDIBLE] child of middle class families would have to go and study piano. I had to go, too. And-- but I didn't like it very much. [LAUGHS] And so one time my teacher, a woman, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] said something like, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] if you really like your mother, that's not enough to just-- to love your mother-- it's not enough to love-- it's not enough to love your mother. You have to do something for her. So you should-- you should practice the piano more, because that's what your mother would like you to do. [LAUGHS] And, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember one time-- we used to go to, to-- in the-- in the summer, we used to go to our grandmother in southern Germany, where my mother was born. And she had a-- she lived in a house that was built in 1736. And that's where she had a store. And the house belonged to her, a nice house. And I still remember that very affectionately, that we stayed there. And all together they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I loved the area and the town and the whole areas of Swabia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where my mother came from, because it was very different. Or I thought it was different, maybe. Maybe it was a more childish feeling that people there were different, friendlier, than up in northern Germany. And the-- the country was flat where I grew up, and there were mountains. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the, the towns were, were more cultured. You know, the whole atmosphere. And I remember one time when we went there when my grandmother was still alive. Usually-- but last-- one time after she had died already, my grandmother had died already, we went there and, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I was looking forward, dreaming of my, my, my-- dreaming of it, you know, looking forward to-- to visiting-- to visit there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we had to change trains from a-- from an express train to a small, small train. And when we got into the small train, there came a group of drunken [? peasants ?] and made a lot of noise. And that already kind of disillusioned me to the south. And, and then we-- instead of going to our grandmother's house, we stayed in a hotel. And when we settled down there, I suddenly started to cry, you know, because I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was probably my first experience with being between illusion and reality, you know? I was still a little kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and one time I went there when I was already order. And we usually went all together, my father, my mother, and my sister and I, on vacations. But in that year for some reason my father and sister didn't go. And so I went alone with my mother to that town. And I remember we had a wonderful time. We hiked in the forest there and, and it was my first experience, I think, in-- with, with great art in Wurzburg, the, the next big town, a bigger town, big town, Wurzburg. There is a palace of, of a bishop and beautiful murals or paintings on the ceilings by a famous Italian master, Tiepolo. And I was so impressed by it. It was really the first time that I saw something like that. And so when-- when we came home after the summer vacations, we always got-- we had to write essays about our vacation. So I wrote about that essay-- I wrote about that experience, about that. And I got a real-- I got an E on that essay. Yeah. And, and I remember that vacation very well with my mother alone. And [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] altogether I was-- I loved my mother very much. And I think she loved me, too. [LAUGHS] And so-- but any more details I really can't recall. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] You know, about essays, writing in school, this is one interesting story. When-- after Hitler came to power, all the teachers had to-- had to adjust their teaching to the Nazi policies. So we had a teacher in German. And he gave a-- he-- he-- and he was a-- he was a Democrat. But he had to do-- in order to keep his job, he had to do what he was told.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he once gave the class [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a topic for an essay. And I don't remember exactly the-- the theme. But it was-- it was a [? theme ?] about nationalism, you know? And more Nazism and so. But for the Jews-- for the Jewish kids-- and, incidentally, at that year, all the Jewish kids were in one class. For us, he gave us different-- a different topic so we didn't have to-- we wouldn't have to write about that. You understand? And-- but I took the theme-- the other theme about nationalism, but I turned it around, and I wrote about Zionism, because Zionism is Jewish-- Jewish nationalism, you know? And so I turned it around and wrote about Zionism as nationalism. And I, I think I also got an E on that. I think I also got an Excellent, an E, an E on that. So I always had to-- had to do things the opposite that-- than, than I was expected to. It didn't make life easier. What are your memories of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was always very critical of me. And whatever I did, criticized it. And it had an effect on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I developed an inferiority complex. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But I don't want to, to talk more, more about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have a sister, who, who lives in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Johanna. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she married. Her married name's Loebl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she an older or younger sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's a year and a half older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you two go to the same school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The-- she went to the same kind of school, but for girls. It wasn't co-ed. The schools were separate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she left Germany after the-- after the Kristallnacht, you know, when all the Jewish stores and all the establishments were destroyed, and ours also. And my father was sent to a concentration camp. And when he came out-- they were-- they were kept for two weeks only. But he-- when he came out, he was broken. He was in, in Buchenwald concentration camp. And they really-- they broke him there. You know, they, they beat them and, and, and marched and tortured them and-- he came out at a-- he had a stroke. He was already around 60. Yeah, 61. And he never recuperated from that. And he died a year later. But before he died, my parents made my sister leave the country. My sister, by the way, was-- before Hitler came to power-- she was engaged to a-- to a Gentile. But [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] very much to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dislike of my parents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But she got engaged to him, and they became used to him. And, and I liked him because he had a beautiful motorbike, you see. I liked my, my sister's fiance because he had a beautiful motorbike, you see. [LAUGHS] And-- but then, then he had to leave town because he was working for the government, and he couldn't be engaged to a Jewish girl having that government job. So he, he got-- he got transferred to another town. And then they met-- and they met clandestinely always. And yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're at the end of the tape.
